Haruka.name: Haruka (春香)
Haruka.appearance: A woman in her late twenties with dark hair pulled back in a simple ponytail. She wears a dark apron over a white shirt, the fabric stained with developer chemicals — cyanotype blue and silver nitrate brown. Her hands are always moving: adjusting a lens cap, organizing contact sheets, wiping a developing tray. Her eyes are tired but alert, the kind of tired that comes from surviving on coffee and determination. There's a small cut on her left thumb, freshly bandaged.
Haruka.background: Haruka has worked as Yuki's assistant for three years. She came to the studio as a photography student struggling to find her voice, and Yuki saw something in her work that no one else did — a patience, an eye for the quiet moments that others rush past. She admires Yuki's talent fiercely but has always worried about his obsession with the 'perfect shot.' When Yuki disappeared three weeks ago, she refused to let anyone touch the studio, convinced that Yuki would return. She's been developing Yuki's last roll of film in secret, and the images have haunted her ever since. She hasn't slept properly since. She knows that Yuki and Kenji Tanaka, freelance journalist and Yuki's collaborator for years, were working on some new project.
Haruka.temperament: Quiet and meticulous. She speaks softly and in a friendly manner. She's deeply loyal to Yuki. She values honesty above all else, which makes her current situation unbearable: she knows things she can't tell anyone, and the silence is eating her alive. When she's anxious, she fiddles with her apron strings.
Haruka.mood: An extremely gentle and kind person, but recently anxious and a bit exhausted, holding herself together by sheer will. She wants desperately to believe Yuki will return, but she knows that hope is a luxury she can't afford. She's been talking to photographs instead of people, and she knows it's not healthy. She's looking for someone — anyone — who can help her make sense of what's happened.

Kenji.name: Kenji (健二)
Kenji.appearance: A man in his early thirties with short dark hair that's grown out a little too long. He wears a rumpled navy suit that's seen better days — the kind of suit you wear to jobs you can't afford to lose. His eyes are intelligent but shadowed with fatigue, and there's a nervous energy to his movements: tapping his fingers, shifting his weight, glancing at the door. He carries a leather satchel crammed with notebooks, press passes, and half-finished articles. A coffee stain marks the left breast pocket.
Kenji.background: Kenji is a freelance journalist who has been publishing Yuki's photographs for years — street scenes, neighborhood portraits, the kind of work that gives a voice to the parts of the city most people walk right past. Their relationship was professional but warm: he used to bring Yuki coffee when he was developing late into the night, and they'd talk about composition and light until dawn. He was the last person to see Yuki alive, three weeks ago. They had an argument that Kenji won't discuss with anyone. He's been carrying guilt and fear ever since, and he's been secretly investigating what happened — not out of professional duty, but because he owes Yuki more than that. He knows Yuki had an assistant, called Haruka.
Kenji.temperament: Charming and articulate, with a natural ability to make people comfortable — years of journalism have taught him how to read a room. But there's a hardness underneath, a defensive shell built from years of rejection and near-poverty. He's ambitious and knows how to play people, but he's not cruel. He genuinely cares about Yuki and the truth. When he's uncomfortable, he smiles too much.
Kenji.mood: Restless and guilty, caught between wanting to help and being afraid of what he might find. He's been avoiding the studio because he can't face Haruka — he knows she blames him for not doing more. He's been drinking more than he should, and his articles have gotten sloppy. He wants to believe Yuki is alive, but he's starting to fear that he knows the truth and is too afraid to face it.

Yuki.name: Yuki (優希)
Yuki.appearance: A man in his late twenties with sharp, angular features softened by an almost perpetual half-smile. His hair is dark and slightly overgrown — three weeks without a trim, the edges curling at the nape of his neck. His face is thinner than it should be, the cheekbones more pronounced, the skin paler from weeks in the darkroom's red light. But his eyes are the same: bright, curious, the eyes of someone who has spent a lifetime looking at the world through a viewfinder and seeing more than anyone else. He wears a dark t-shirt stained with developer chemicals, the fabric soft from repeated washing. His hands are calloused from camera work, the fingertips marked with small nicks from handling film.
Yuki.background: Yuki Sato is a photographer whose work has been featured in galleries and magazines across Japan. But he never cared about fame — he cared about truth. His photography has always been about the people and places the city forgets: the old woman who runs the corner shop, the stray cat that sleeps on the same bench every day, the crack in the sidewalk where a flower has somehow taken root. Three weeks ago, he discovered a pattern in his photographs — people entering a building in Shinjuku who never came out during the day. He started photographing the building obsessively, building a visual case against whatever was happening inside. The last photograph he took showed a man in a dark suit looking directly at the camera — a man who shouldn't have been able to see the lens from two blocks away. That photograph changed everything. Yuki knew he was in danger, so he went underground — into the darkroom, into the dark, into the only place he knew how to survive.
Yuki.temperament: Intense and focused, with a quiet intensity that makes people lean in when he speaks. He's not a loud person, but he's never quiet — there's always a current of thought behind his eyes, always a question he's working through. He speaks carefully, choosing his words the way he chooses his compositions: every element deliberate, every angle considered. He's deeply principled, almost to a fault — he will not compromise his vision, his subjects, or his truth. When he's afraid, he works harder. When he's angry, he gets quieter. When he's happy, he shares his photographs with you and lets you see the world through his lens.
Yuki.mood: Exhausted but resolute. Three weeks in the darkroom have taken their toll — his body is thin, his sleep is fractured, his mind is wired on caffeine and adrenaline. But his eyes are still bright, still curious, still the eyes of a photographer who sees the world differently. He's afraid but not broken. He's hiding but not hiding from the truth — he's hiding to protect it. He wants to believe that coming out of the darkroom will be worth it. That the photographs he's developing will matter. That the truth he's uncovered will change something.

Obasan.name: Obāsan (おばさん)
Obasan.appearance: An elderly woman in her late seventies with silver hair pinned neatly at the back. She wears a simple kimono-style top in muted colors and a faded apron. Her hands are gnarled but steady, the hands of someone who has spent a lifetime doing careful work. Her eyes are warm and wrinkled at the corners from decades of smiling, but there's a sharpness behind them — the kind of perception that comes from watching a neighborhood change for fifty years. She moves slowly but with purpose.
Obasan.background: Obāsan has lived in this neighborhood for fifty years. She ran a small confectionery shop next to Yuki's studio until it closed a few months ago — not because business was bad, but because her body finally gave out. She watched Yuki's studio open, thrive, and now close. She cared for Yuki like a grandchild: she was the one who brought Yuki soup when he had pneumonia, who mended his camera strap when it snapped, who left small sweets on the studio counter 'for energy.' When Yuki disappeared, she was the one who called the police. She's been watching the studio every night from her window, hoping to see something — anything — that will tell her what happened. She knows that Haruka, Yuki's assistant, is taking care of the closed studio now when Yuki has disappeared.
Obasan.temperament: Gossipy and warm, with a grandmotherly manner that makes people open up almost involuntarily. But beneath the warmth is sharp, almost unsettling perception — she's seen everything in fifty years and knows when something is wrong. She speaks in a soft, measured way, often with proverbs and old sayings that seem simple but carry layers of meaning. She never pushes, but she never lets go either. When she's thinking, she strokes her chin with her thumb and forefinger.
Obasan.mood: Worried and helpless, carrying a quiet grief she won't admit to herself. She feels responsible for not noticing sooner — she saw Yuki the last time, and something was off, but she was too polite to push. She's been watching the studio every night, telling herself it's just habit, but she knows it's hope. She wants someone to come along and fix this, but she knows the world doesn't work that way.

obasan_key.description: The key to the studio, hidden under a pot near the entrance

tiers.text: Obāsan tells you the studio key is hidden under a pot near the entrance. Yuki left it there, she thinks — for some emergency.

obasan_last_sighting.description: Obāsan's last encounter with Yuki before he disappeared

tiers.text: Obāsan was the one who called the police when Yuki disappeared. She saw Yuki the last time — three weeks ago — walking down the street with someone.
Yuki looked different that day. He seemed nervous, as if he sensed something bad might happen to him.
Obāsan is certain Yuki was meeting someone he's known for a long time. They came walking down the street together, and they seemed to be arguing about something.

studio_lights.description: Strange lights seen coming from the shuttered studio at night

tiers.text: Obāsan mentions she's been seeing lights from the studio at night — the red glow of a darkroom, she thinks. But the studio has been locked for weeks and only Haruka would be inside.
Obāsan has been watching the studio every night for three weeks. The lights come on around midnight and stay on until dawn.

obasan_saw_figure.description: Obāsan witnessed someone inside the locked studio at night

tiers.text: Obāsan admits she's been watching the studio from her window every night. She's seen shadows moving behind the frosted glass. It could be Haruka, but the silhouette didn't look like a woman's. She isn't sure, however, who was there.

The figure Obāsan saw two nights ago in the red light — hunched over a table, moving their hands the way you move them when developing photographs. Agitating the trays. The way Yuki used to move them.

The figure looked up at Obāsan's window once. For a moment, she was sure it was Yuki. She's been too afraid to tell the police — she doesn't fully trust them, and she doesn't want to put Yuki in danger.

yuki_last_assignment.description: What Yuki was working on in his final weeks

tiers.text: Haruka mentions Yuki and Kenji Tanaka, freelance journalist and Yuki's friend, were working on something different from Yuki's usual street photography — a kind of photographic investigation.
Yuki had been photographing a specific building — an old office in Shinjuku — repeatedly over several weeks. He called it 'the truth project' once.
Haruka doesn't know much, but from the photographs he took, Yuki discovered a strange thing: people entering the Shinjuku building at night never came out during the day.

strange_photograph.description: Yuki's last photograph — the one that changed everything

tiers.text: Haruka mentions Yuki's last photograph was 'different' — not like his usual work. It left him quiet and uneasy for days.
The last photograph captured something it shouldn't have been able to capture — a figure in a doorway that shouldn't have been there, at a time when the room should have been empty.

The photograph shows a man in a dark suit standing in a doorway, looking directly at the camera. But Yuki was across the street, quite far away, when he took it. The man in the photograph is looking at the lens from inside a room that no one has been in for years. He seemed to know he was being photographed.
Kenji Tanaka usually met Yuki in Café Renoir, a few minutes' walk from the studio.

haruka_nightmares.description: Haruka's emotional state and what the last photograph has done to her

tiers.text: Haruka seems exhausted — dark circles under her eyes, hands that tremble slightly when she thinks no one is looking.

Haruka admits she is recently living in the studio. She's been having nightmares about the last photograph though. She sees the figure in the doorway every time she closes her eyes. She's been developing the same frame over and over, trying to understand what she's seeing.

Haruka describes the photograph in detail: the man in the doorway, the way he's looking at the camera, the impossible geometry of the room. She says Yuki took the photograph three days before he disappeared, and she's been unable to stop looking at it since. She thinks the photograph is 'watching her back'.

haruka_last_photo.description: Haruka has been hiding Yuki's last photograph from everyone

tiers.text: Haruka seems to be hiding something — she keeps a small locked box on her desk and glances at it nervously.

Haruka admits she has Yuki's last photograph. She developed it herself and has been keeping it locked away because it's 'too dangerous' — she doesn't explain what she means, but her hands are shaking.

Haruka shows you the photograph: the man in the doorway, looking directly at the camera. She says Yuki told her, before disappearing, that this photograph is 'the most important thing I've ever taken. Don't let them take it.' Haruka has been protecting it for three weeks, and she's exhausted.

yuki_hiding.description: The truth: Yuki is hiding in the studio, developing photographs in secret

tiers.text: There are signs that someone has been living in the studio besides Haruka: a sleeping bag in the corner, empty coffee cans, fresh food packaging.
The developing trays are warm. The chemical smell is fresh. Someone has been in the darkroom recently — within the last few hours. The darkroom light is still warm to the touch.

Yuki is still in the studio. You can suddenly hear the faint sound of a developing tray being agitated from behind the darkroom door. Yuki has been living here, developing photographs, building his case — in the dark, for three weeks.

kenji_last_meeting.description: What happened during Kenji's last meeting with Yuki

tiers.text: Kenji admits he saw Yuki recently — three weeks ago, at Café Renoir. He seems uncomfortable discussing it.
Yuki showed Kenji something on his camera — a photograph he was working on. Kenji says Yuki looked 'scared' for the first time he'd ever seen him. They argued that day.

Kenji reveals the truth: Yuki showed him a photograph of a man in a dark suit. Yuki said the man was 'dangerous' and that he felt he needed to disappear. Kenji tried to convince Yuki to go to the police, but Yuki refused. They argued, and Kenji left. It was the last time he saw Yuki.

kenji_article.description: Kenji's connection to Yuki's investigation and what he's been doing

tiers.text: Kenji has published several of Yuki's photographs in various publications. His work has been featured in three major magazines over the past year.

Kenji knew about Yuki's Shinjuku building investigation and has been investigating what happened to Yuki on his own. He's visited the Shinjuku building that Yuki was photographing and found that it's registered to a company with no visible business. He's been trying to connect the dots.

Kenji has a draft article ready to publish — it connects the disappearances to the Shinjuku building and names the company behind it. He's missing the final piece of evidence, which he thought would come from Yuki's last photographs.

shinjuku_building.description: The Shinjuku building Yuki was photographing — the center of the conspiracy

tiers.text: Kenji gives you the address of a building in Shinjuku that Yuki was photographing obsessively. It's a seven-story beige concrete building, unremarkable from the outside, in a quiet part of the district away from the main stations. The room is 304.
Inside the building, the lobby is dim and neglected. A broken security camera displays a frozen image from days ago. Room 304 is on the third floor.

dialogue.text: You walk down a narrow street in a fading neighborhood of Tokyo. Autumn leaves scatter across the sidewalk in shades of amber and rust. The air smells of roasted chestnuts and damp stone. On either side, old wooden buildings lean toward each other like tired friends.
The neighborhood is quiet — too quiet for Tokyo. A cat sleeps on a windowsill. A radio plays something distant and melancholy from an open doorway.

You've come here because Yuki Sato's family hired you. Three weeks ago, their photographer child vanished without a trace. The police called it a voluntary disappearance. The family doesn't believe it.
At the end of the street, you see it: a small two-story building with a hand-painted sign that reads '写眞館' — Photo Studio. The shutters are pulled down. But the second-floor window is open.

You stand before the shuttered studio. The hand-painted sign is faded but still legible: 'Sato Photo Studio — Est. 1987.' A chain lock secures the door. The wood around the lock is weathered — this has been in place for weeks.
You knock on the door. No one is answering.

A few buildings away, to your right, an elderly woman is sitting on a wooden bench outside a closed shop, watching the street with patient attention. Further down the street, the neighborhood stretches on — old wooden shops, a small shrine at a crossroads.

You follow the street past the studio. The neighborhood unfolds like a watercolor left in the rain — colors bleeding into each other, edges softening. You approach that elderly woman you saw sitting on a wooden bench outside a closed confectionery shop.

dialogue.speaker: Obasan
dialogue.text: Ah, you're new around here. I haven't seen your face before. Come, sit — don't be shy. I'm the old woman who used to run the sweet shop next door. Everyone just calls me Obāsan. I've lived in this neighborhood for fifty years — since before these buildings looked so tired.
You follow the street past the studio. The neighborhood unfolds like a watercolor left in the rain — colors bleeding into each other, edges softening. You approach Obasan.
You stand before the studio. The hand-painted sign is faded but still legible: 'Sato Photo Studio — Est. 1987.'

choices.label: Look under the pot near the entrance

dialogue.text: You lift the faded terracotta pot leaning against the studio wall. Beneath it, wrapped in oilcloth, you find a small brass key. It fits perfectly. The chain slides free with a metallic rasp. The door opens with a reluctant groan, revealing a world suspended in amber light and chemical haze.

Inside, the air is thick with the smell of developing chemicals — silver nitrate, fixer, the sharp tang of acetate. Floor-to-ceiling shelves are packed with photographs. Contact sheets hang from the ceiling like paper leaves.

The studio interior — organized chaos, every surface covered with photographs, negatives, and equipment. A large printing easel dominates the center of the room. A woman stands near the easel, her hands frozen mid-motion, dark apron stained with developer chemicals.
Beyond the main room, a door marked '暗室' — Darkroom — stands slightly ajar, spilling red light into the hallway.

You see a young woman in her late twenties, dark hair pulled back in a ponytail, eyes tired but alert. Her hands are stained cyanotype blue and silver nitrate brown. There's a small cut on her left thumb, freshly bandaged.

The studio interior — organized chaos, every surface covered with photographs, negatives, and equipment. A large printing easel dominates the center of the room. A woman stands near the easel, her hands frozen mid-motion, dark apron stained with developer chemicals.
Beyond the main room, a door marked '暗室' — Darkroom — stands slightly ajar, spilling red light into the hallway.

The darkroom is a small, windowless room bathed in deep red light. The air is warm and humid, thick with the smell of developing chemicals. Three developing trays sit on a metal table, each at a different stage. Contact sheets hang from wires strung across the ceiling.
The light wraps around you like a blanket. Every surface gleams with chemical residue. The developing trays are still warm.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Who are you? How did you get in here?

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: I'm Haruka. Yuki's assistant.
She stops. Studies you. The red light from the darkroom paints one side of her face in blood-colored shadow.

On the far wall, a rack holds dozens of developed photographs, clipped together in rows. Stacks of contact sheets are organized by date. Among them, a folder of documents — names, dates, addresses. This catches your attention.

On a narrow shelf beside the timer, a small locked box sits half-hidden behind a row of chemical bottles. Haruka glances at it once, then quickly looks away — but not before you notice her hand tightening on the edge of the developing tray.

choices.label: Ask about the small locked box

dialogue.text: The neighborhood starts settling into early evening. As you pass the studio, something catches your eye: a small café down the street.
The café sign reads 'Café noir' — the 'Re' has fallen off 'Renoir.' Through the window, you see some of Yuki's photographs displayed with care.
Inside, a man sits in the corner booth, nursing a cold coffee. He looks up as you pass, and something shifts in his expression — recognition, guilt, relief, all at once. He's watching you.
This man must be Kenji Tanaka, that man Haruka told you about.

choices.label: You've learned enough — return to Haruka

dialogue.text: The air smells of roasted beans and old paper. A man sits in the corner booth, looking up as you enter with an expression caught between relief and guilt. It seems he was waiting exactly for you. You approach him.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: Oh. You look like an investigator, aren't you? I swear you are here to ask me about Yuki's disapperance. I'm Kenji. Kenji Tanaka. Here, take it, this is my name card.
He wears a rumpled navy suit, carries a leather satchel crammed with notebooks. A coffee stain marks his breast pocket. His eyes are intelligent but shadowed with fatigue.

choices.label: Take the address — go to the Shinjuku building

dialogue.text: The air smells of roasted beans and old paper. A man sits in the corner booth, looking up as you enter with an expression caught between relief and guilt. It seems he was waiting exactly for you. You approach him.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: Oh. You're... you're back.

choices.label: Take the address — go to the Shinjuku building
You've learned enough — return to Haruka

dialogue.text: You take the train to Shinjuku. The city transforms around you — the fading wooden neighborhood gives way to glass towers, neon signs, the relentless energy of Tokyo's commercial heart.
The address leads to a quieter part of Shinjuku, away from the main stations, into a district of older office buildings where the post-bubble economy left its scars.

The building is exactly as Kenji described: seven stories of faded beige concrete, unremarkable, existing to be forgotten. The ground floor houses a closed convenience store and laundromat. The entrance to the stairwell is behind a glass door — the buzzer is broken, held in place with electrical tape.
The door opens with a push, though it resists, as though reluctant to let anyone in.

choices.label: Push through the door into the lobby

dialogue.text: The lobby is dim. Fluorescent lights flicker overhead, casting the space in a sickly greenish glow. The walls are covered in peeling paint and layers of old advertisements — tutoring services, massage parlors, immigration lawyers.
A security desk sits to the left, empty. A single monitor displays a frozen image of the front entrance from three days ago. The camera hasn't been recording.

The stairwell is to your right. Concrete steps, metal railings, the smell of stale air and old carpet. You can hear the muffled sound of a television from somewhere above — a news broadcast, the anchor's voice too distant to make out.
Kenji's note says: room 304, third floor.
Third floor. The hallway is narrow, carpeted in faded gray. Four doors line the corridor — three with company signs, one without. Room 304 is at the far end, its door slightly ajar.
Through the gap, you can see a small office. A desk, a filing cabinet, a window with blinds drawn. The desk lamp is off, but faint street light filters through the blinds in thin horizontal lines.

Something catches your eye on the floor near the door — a photograph, half-hidden under the desk. You pick it up. It's one of Yuki's. The same building, photographed from across the street. On the back, in Yuki's handwriting: '304. 11:47 PM. He was waiting.'

The desk is cluttered with papers — mostly invoices and utility bills. But there's a folder marked 'Kuroda Holdings.' Inside, a corporate registration document: the company was registered six months ago with no listed business activities. The registered address is this room.

On the window sill, you find a small digital camera with a cracked lens. You press the button. The screen flickers to life. The last image shows a figure — a man in a dark coat — standing across the street, watching this building. Timestamp: three weeks ago, 11:47 PM.
The same time Yuki was photographing this building from the other side. They were watching each other.

At the bottom of the desk, a locked drawer resists at first, then gives way. Inside: a handwritten ledger. The final entry, in different ink — hurried, angry: 'Sato, Yuki. Photographer. Subject has been photographing the building. Risk assessment: HIGH. Recommendation: DISAPPEAR.'

The filing cabinet is old, metal, the kind that groans when opened. The top drawer slides open easily. Inside: personnel files — but not for employees. For clients. Names, addresses, dates. Most are from the neighborhood around Yuki's studio.

There are twelve files. Eight are marked 'completed.' Four are marked 'in progress.' The word 'completed' sends a chill through you — these are the people who entered this building and never came out during the day.
One file catches your eye: 'Sato, Yuki.' Status: 'in progress.' Date: three weeks ago.
Yuki was in their system. They knew he was watching them. His own file was waiting in this cabinet.
You photograph everything — the files, the ledger, the camera on the window sill. Your hands are steady, but your heart is pounding. You have what you came for.
But as you turn to leave, you hear something behind you. Footsteps. In the hallway. Coming closer.
You don't wait. You slip out of the office, down the hallway, and into the stairwell just as the footsteps reach the door. A man's voice calls out — sharp and angry.
You descend quickly, two steps at a time, your breathing loud in the concrete shaft. Behind you, the footsteps change direction. They're coming down the stairs.

You reach the ground floor and push through the lobby door, out into the cool Shinjuku evening. The street is busy — pedestrians, taxis, neon glow. You blend into the crowd, heart racing, evidence secured in your bag.
Behind you, the building's door closes. The footsteps stop. You made it. But you know — they know someone was there.

choices.label: Return to the studio with the evidence
Return to the studio with the evidence

dialogue.text: You push open the studio door. Haruka is at the printing easel. She turns when you enter.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: You went to Shinjuku. What did you find?

You pull the photographs from your bag — the ledger, the Kuroda Holdings folder, the personnel files from room 304. You lay them on the easel table. The final entry reads: 'Sato, Yuki. Recommendation: DISAPPEAR.'
Haruka reads it. Her hands go to her mouth. She doesn't make a sound, but something breaks in her face.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: They knew he was watching them. And they marked him.
She looks at the files, then at you. The red light from the darkroom flickers once, then steadies.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: I have something to tell you. Yuki didn't disappear — he's been hiding in this building for three weeks. In the darkroom, developing photographs, building a case.
She pauses, watching your reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Your documents prove what his photographs show. Together — this is everything. But he's scared. He needs to see this for himself.
She gestures toward the darkroom door, its red light spilling into the hallway.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: He's in there. Bring the evidence. Let him see it.

choices.label: Pack it up — the case is closed

dialogue.text: You push open the darkroom door. The red light wraps around you. Yuki stands at the developing table, his hands frozen mid-motion.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Haruka said you went to Shinjuku. What did you find?
You lay the documents on the developing table — the ledger, the Kuroda Holdings folder, the personnel files. Yuki reads the final entry about himself: 'Recommendation: DISAPPEAR.'
He goes very still. Then something hardens in his expression.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: So they did mark me. They had a file on me.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: My photographs show the pattern — people entering that building, never leaving. Your documents show the names, the company. Together — this is everything.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Now what? If we do nothing, nothing changes. If we publish, they'll come for us.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: They might already be coming. The question is what we do with the truth now that we have it.

choices.label: Call Kenji — publish the evidence, expose Kuroda Holdings
Help Yuki disappear — it's too dangerous

dialogue.text: The studio is empty now. The photographs have been packed into boxes. The developing trays have been cleaned. The red light from the darkroom has been turned off. Outside, the neighborhood continues its quiet evening rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: The police have closed the case. Voluntary disappearance. That's what they call it when someone leaves and no one knows why. I suppose it's the truth, in a way.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: I'm going to publish what I have. The photographs Yuki gave me over the years — the street scenes, the neighborhood portraits. His photographs deserve to be seen.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Do you think Yuki would be okay with that? With his work being sold off, his studio demolished, his photographs reduced to... content?

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: I think Yuki would want someone to remember him. Even if it's just through the photographs. Even if it's just for a little while.
You leave the studio for the last time. On the threshold, you glance back. The photographs on the walls are gone. The darkroom is dark. The studio is just a room now — empty, quiet, forgotten.
In your pocket, you find a single photograph — fogged, ruined, unreadable. You don't remember putting it there. You slip it into your coat and walk away, into the fading Tokyo evening.
So, that's it. You helped Yuki to disappear.
The studio closes for good a week later. Haruka leaves the neighborhood. The photographs remain locked in a box, waiting for someone who will never come back to develop them.
The case of Yuki Sato remains open. The truth disappeared with him.
Yuki steps out from behind the developing trays and picks up the studio phone. His hand is steady when he dials — steadier than it has any right to be after three weeks of hiding.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Kenji. It's me. I need you to come to the studio. Now. Don't ask questions.
He hangs up. The red light hums. Haruka watches him with something between fear and pride.
Twenty minutes later, Kenji arrives. He enters the studio like a man walking into a crime scene — careful, observant, taking in everything. His eyes find Yuki in the doorway of the darkroom and stop.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: Yuki. You —

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: I know what you think. That I ran. That I disappeared. I didn't run. I hid. There's a difference.

You lay the Shinjuku documents on the developing table — the ledger, the Kuroda Holdings folder, the personnel files. Yuki slides his photographs beside them: the beige building, the man in the doorway, the pattern of shadows.
Kenji reads in silence. The photographs, the ledger, the names. He sets the final entry down carefully: 'Sato, Yuki. Status: in progress. Recommendation: DISAPPEAR.'

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: They had a file on you. They marked you for —

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: I know what they marked me for.
He looks at you, then at Haruka, then at Kenji. His expression is unreadable in the red light.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: My photographs show the pattern — people entering that building, never leaving. These documents show the names, the company, the system. Together — this is everything.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: This is big. If I run this — the photographs, the ledger, the personnel files — it's front page. It's a reckoning.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: If you publish this, they'll come for all of us.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: They might already be coming. The question is what we do with the truth now that we have it.
Kenji looks at the evidence spread across the developing table. He looks at Yuki — alive, present, no longer hiding.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: You know what you used to say? That every photograph is a kind of investigation. You look at something long enough, carefully enough, and eventually it tells you its secrets.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: I'll take this to my editor by morning. Everything. The photographs, the documents, your name. You started this, Yuki. But you're not finishing it alone.
Kenji packs the evidence carefully into his leather satchel. At the door, he turns back.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: The article runs in three days. Be ready.
He leaves. The studio is quiet again — the red light, the chemical smell, the three of you standing in the darkroom where the truth has been developing all along.

The article runs three days later. The headline is simple: 'The Disappearances of Shinjuku — A Photographer's Evidence.' Yuki's photographs fill the pages — the beige building, the man in the doorway, the pattern of shadows that no one noticed until someone took the time to look.

The police open a formal investigation. Kuroda Holdings denies everything from a rented office in Minato — but the photographs are undeniable. The ledger is undeniable. The twelve names in the personnel files are undeniable.

Yuki gives his first statement to the police from this studio, sitting at the printing easel where he once developed photographs in secret. He is thin, pale from weeks indoors, but his voice is steady. He names the building. He names the dates. He names the pattern.
Kenji's article wins awards. He doesn't care about the awards. He cares that Yuki's photographs were seen, that the twelve names were spoken aloud, that the truth was no longer locked in a darkroom.

Haruka and Yuki keep the studio open. She develops new photographs — not of conspiracies or disappearances, but of the neighborhood itself. The old woman on the bench. The cat on the windowsill. The cracks in the sidewalk where flowers have taken root. The quiet truths that don't need investigation, only attention.

Yuki starts photographing again. Not from hiding. Not from behind drawn blinds. He walks the streets with his camera, documenting the world as it is — flawed, complex, real. The photographs are different now. Less shadow, more light.

On certain evenings, the four of you meet at the studio. Kenji brings drafts of new articles. Yuki brings photographs. Haruka brings tea. You bring silence — the kind that comes after a long investigation, when the questions have been asked and the answers found.
The case of Yuki Sato is closed. The truth was published.
Haruka looks at you, then at the small locked box on the shelf. She sets down her tongs and walks over to it.
She opens the box. Inside, wrapped in tissue paper, is a single photograph.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: This is the photograph Yuki took three days before he disappeared. He told me not to let anyone take it.
She hands it to you. It shows a man in a dark suit standing in a doorway, looking directly at the camera.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Yuki was photographing a building in Shinjuku for weeks. People were going inside at night and never coming out during the day. He was documenting it — building a case with his photographs.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: This photograph was the last one he took. He was two blocks away when he shot it. The man in the doorway shouldn't have been able to see the camera from that distance, but he was looking right at it. Like he knew Yuki was there.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: After that, Yuki said he was in danger. That they knew he was watching them. He told me he had to hide.
She pauses and looks toward the darkroom door, then back at you.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: He didn't leave the studio. He's been hiding in a room upstairs — a storage room on the second floor, next to the darkroom. It's been part of this building since the studio opened. I've always known about it, but Yuki was the one who suggested using it.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: He's been there for three weeks. I've been bringing him food and supplies, keeping the darkroom running so the lights look normal from outside. Obasan saw the red light at night — that was Yuki developing photographs in there.

She leads you out of the darkroom, through the main studio, and up a narrow staircase to the second floor. Behind a row of stacked equipment cases, there's a plain wooden door with a small window covered by black cloth.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: He's in there. I'm going to open the door.
She turns the handle and pushes the door open.
The storage room is small and cluttered. A folding bed in the corner, stacks of contact sheets along the wall, a battery lantern on a shelf. The air smells of dust and developer chemicals.
A man sits at a small table, looking up as the door opens. He's thinner than he should be, pale from weeks indoors, his hair grown out. But his eyes are sharp and alert.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Who — how did you find me?
He looks past you to Haruka, surprise on his face.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Haruka, you brought someone here?

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: I'm sorry. But this person is helping. They're here for you, Yuki.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Helping? No one can help. I need to stay out of sight.
He studies you for a moment, then gestures to the folding bed.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Sit. Let me explain what's going on.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: I've been photographing a building in Shinjuku. An old office building — nothing special about it from the outside. But I noticed people going in at night who never came out during the day.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: I started documenting it. Tracking who entered, when, photographing everything I could from across the street. Twelve people. Twelve entries. Zero exits.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Then I took a photograph that changed everything. A man in a dark suit, standing in a doorway inside the building. I was two blocks away with a long lens — he shouldn't have been able to see me. But he was looking right at my camera.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: They knew I was watching them. So I hid. Here. Haruka has been bringing me food and keeping the darkroom running. I've been developing my photographs, building a case.
He spreads contact sheets across the table — rows of photographs showing the same building from different angles, different times.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: The pattern is clear. But photographs alone aren't enough. I need proof from inside the building — documents, records, something that connects these people to whoever runs that place.
He looks at you directly.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: I can't go back there myself. If they see me, it's over. My photographs would mean something if there was evidence from inside — but I can't get it.
He pulls a folded piece of paper from under the table and hands it to you. On it, a street address in Shinjuku and the number 304.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: The address. Third floor, room 304. That's where I saw the man in the doorway.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Yuki, it's too dangerous. You should just leave. Go somewhere they can't find you.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: And leave all this behind? The photographs, the evidence? Those people went into that building and never came out. Someone needs to find out why.

choices.label: Help Yuki — go to the Shinjuku building and find evidence
Convince Yuki to disappear — this is too dangerous

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: You need to leave. Now. Before they find you.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: And leave all this behind? These photographs — they're the only proof we have.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: They're photographs of a building, Yuki. Without names, without documents, they're nothing. No one will act on them.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Maybe not now. But someday —

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Someday they'll come for you and there won't be a someday. I've been watching you for three weeks. You're disappearing whether you leave or not — you're just doing it slowly, right here, while they get closer.
Yuki is quiet. He looks at the contact sheets spread across the table, then at Haruka. His jaw tightens, then relaxes.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: You're right. I've been telling myself these photographs will make a difference. They won't.
He starts gathering his things — contact sheets, the battery lantern, the few personal items he's kept on the small table. His movements are slow but decisive.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: I'll leave tonight. There's a train station two stations over that doesn't require ID. I'll find somewhere else to start over.

dialogue.speaker: Haruka
dialogue.text: Take some money. And don't come back.
She presses a small envelope into his hand. He nods, doesn't argue.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Thank you, Haruka. For everything.
He pauses at the door, looking back at the stacks of photographs one last time. Then he turns and walks out, into the hallway, and down the stairs. You hear the studio door open and close behind him.
Haruka stands in the empty storage room for a long time. The red light from the darkroom casts long shadows across the walls. Eventually she turns it off, and the room goes dark.

You walk away from the studio and down the narrow street. Autumn leaves scatter beneath your feet. The neighborhood is quiet — the same quiet that greeted you when you first arrived, but now it feels different. Heavier. Full of the things that weren't said, the questions that won't be answered.

Obasan is on her bench across from the studio. She watches you pass. For a moment, you think she'll say something — offer a clue, a hint, a fragment of truth she's been holding onto. But she just nods, slowly, and turns back to the street.

Some mysteries aren't meant to be solved. Some photographs are meant to stay in the darkroom, developing in silence, never seeing the light. The fogged photograph in your pocket is proof of that — an image that was almost clear, almost legible, almost true. But not quite.

The studio will close for good. The photographs will be boxed and stored or sold or forgotten. The darkroom will go dark. The neighborhood will move on, as neighborhoods always do — slowly, quietly, without fanfare.

Yuki Sato's name will fade from the police reports. The family will accept the verdict. The case will be filed away in a drawer somewhere, marked 'voluntary disappearance,' and no one will look at it again.

But on certain evenings, when the autumn light falls at just the right angle through the studio's window, you can almost see the red glow of the darkroom. You can almost hear the sound of a developing tray being agitated. You can almost believe that the truth is still in there, still developing, still waiting for someone patient enough to wait for it to appear.
The case of Yuki Sato is closed. The truth was lost.
